back_translation: At the city of Iconium, Paul and Barnabas entered the synagogue of the Jews as before, and spoke words in such a manner that a great number of both Jews and Greeks came to believe.
But the Jews who refused to believe stirred up and caused the minds of the Gentiles to think evil against the brothers.
Nevertheless, the two spent a considerable time there, speaking boldly relying on the Lord, who confirmed the word of His grace, granting signs and wonders to occur by the hands of the two.
But the people of the city were divided — some sided with the Jews, and others with the apostles.
But when both the Gentiles and the Jews, together with their rulers, moved together to mistreat and to stone the two,
when the two became aware, they fled to the city of Lystra and the city of Derbe in the province of Lycaonia and to the surrounding territory,
and there the two continued preaching the good news.
At the city of Lystra there sat a certain man who was crippled in his feet, disabled from birth, who had never walked.
This man listened to Paul speaking. Paul gazed intently at him and saw that he had faith to receive healing.
So Paul said with a loud voice, 'Stand up straight on your feet!' And the man jumped up and began to walk.
When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they raised their voices in the Lycaonian language saying, 'The gods have transformed themselves into men and have come down to us!'
They called Barnabas 'Zeus,' and Paul they called 'Hermes,' because Paul was the leader in speaking.
The priest of the temple of Zeus, which stood before the city, brought male bulls and garlands to the city gates, desiring to offer a sacrifice together with the crowds.
But when the apostles — that is, Barnabas and Paul — heard of this matter, the two tore their own clothes and rushed swiftly into the crowd, crying out,

saying, 'You all, why are you doing this? We too are ordinary humans just like you. We have come to proclaim the good news to you so that you would abandon these worthless things and turn back to the living God, who made the heavens, the earth, the sea, and all things in them.'
In past generations that have gone by, He permitted all nations to walk in their own ways.
Yet He did not leave Himself without witness — that is, doing good, granting rains from the sky to you and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts with food and gladness.'
Even though the two spoke in this manner, they barely stopped the crowds from offering sacrifices to them.
But then Jews from the city of Antioch and the city of Iconium arrived. They persuaded the crowds to agree, then stoned Paul with stones and dragged him outside the city, thinking that Paul had died.
But when the disciples came and surrounded Paul, he stood up and went back into the city. The next day he departed with Barnabas for the city of Derbe.
When the two had preached the good news in that city and made many disciples, they returned to the city of Lystra, the city of Iconium, and the city of Antioch,
strengthening the hearts of the disciples, urging them to remain firm in the faith, and saying, 'We all must pass through many hardships before we can enter into the kingdom of God.'
The two appointed church-elders in each church for them, and when they had prayed and fasted, they entrusted them to the Lord, in whom they had trusted.
The two traveled through the province of Pisidia and arrived at the province of Pamphylia.
When the two had proclaimed the word at the city of Perga, they went down to the city of Attalia.
From there the two sailed to the city of Antioch, where the two had been entrusted to the grace of God for the work that now had been completed.
When the two arrived and gathered the church together, they reported all things that God had done together with them, and how He had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.
And the two stayed there with the disciples for a long time.
